text:
The project investigates the quality of advocacy in proceedings brought against young people, and identifies the key elements of effective advocacy practice. The Bar Standards Board is using the findings of the research to help it decide whether to impose regulatory measures on young people who appear before the courts. The research was a multi-method exercise, based on a survey of 215 advocates, interviews with 96 young people, victims, magistrates, District Judges and members of Youth Offending Teams, and observation of cases in four Youth Courts and five Crown Courts in England and Wales.

A Cautionary Tale: Quality of Representation in the Youth Court The findings highlight the varying quality of advocacy observed throughout the week. In order to provide effective advocacy in the Youth Court, advocates will require: A fundamental understanding of the law governing youth justice and child development An ability to communicate and connect with young people through the use of language A high level of professionalism is also expected. However, a number of hindrances to achieving these standards have been highlighted, including: The YCT being viewed as a “learning curve” for young and even moderately experienced advocates; defendants not being identified as requiring specific support (as illustrated by the lack of advocate/client consultation occurring prior to hearings); and the impact of low funded Legal Aid.

Youth Justice and By-law Advocacy in Ontario According to the report it is not possible to determine the effectiveness of individual advocates independent of the broader legal, institutional and cultural context of the youth justice system. Recommendations for improving the effectiveness of court-based youth justice and by-law advocacy include making youth justice advocacy a formal specialism with a regulatory or licensing body to govern its practice and to ensure a uniform national standard for the delivery of court-based youth justice and by-law services. A number of recommendations for increasing opportunities for children to engage with the courts and for enhancing the competence of child advocates and court-based practitioners have also been made as have recommendations for mandatory, activity-based training and learning for child advocates.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
